body_txt: Most people react to a dangerous situation (the sudden appearance of Giganto the Stomper, for example) with some degree of fear, anger, or resignation . Then there's Bob, who giggles and rubs his hands for sheer glee. Why? Well, he's done a lot of research on Giganto. He's always wanted to see it. He was the one person who believed it existed, and now he's been vindicated. Whatever the case, he's absolutely thrilled that Giganto is here and close enough to kill him. Of course, the fanboy trance usually fades when Bob realizes the implications of the whole "close enough to kill him" thing. In serious works, his encyclopedic knowledge gives him the means to neutralize the threat, or at least get himself and everyone else out of harm's way. In more comedic works or works in which It Can Think , he could even graduate to full-fledged Fluffy Tamer and befriend Giganto... though Giganto might still be willing to stomp on the rest of the team. Alternatively, Bob never realizes he is in danger and skates out unharmed through blind luck. Obviously, while this usually applies to fictional fantasy dangers, this can also apply to threats someone might encounter in Real Life , especially natural dangers like tornadoes, tigers, or sharks. Can be a particularly disturbing example of Complimenting the Transformed . Compare Villain Respect , Worthy Opponent , Misaimed Fandom , Nightmare Fetishist , Casual Danger Dialogue , In Love with Your Carnage , Measuring the Marigolds , Ugly Cute , The Xenophile , Obsessed with Deadly Item , and Arson, Murder, and Admiration . Contrast Wowing Cthulhu , where the abomination admires you , and Horror Struck , where Bob was not expecting Giganto to be real, and finding proof of its existence brings his brain to a screeching halt.
